Material risks (from 10-K Item 1A): Macro-Economic Factors: Adverse changes in the U.S. securities markets, a higher interest rate environment, rising inflation and medical costs could materially increase our benefit plan costs and future funding requirements.
Significant adverse changes in capital markets could result in the deterioration of our defined benefit plans’ funded status.

Inflationary pressures on costs, such as inputs for devices we sell and network components, labor and distribution costs, may impact our network construction, our financial condition or results of operations.
Inflationary and supply pressures may continue into the future and could have an adverse impact on our ability to source materials.
Our attempts to offset these cost and supply pressures, such as through increases in the selling prices of some of our products and services, may not be successful.

Consumers may be less willing to pay a price differential for our products and services and may increasingly purchase lower-priced offerings from us or our competitors, or may forego some purchases altogether, during a period of inflationary pressure or an economic downturn.

To the extent that price increases are not sufficient to offset these increased costs adequately or in a timely manner, and/or if they result in significant decreases in sales volume, our business, financial condition or operating results may be adversely affected.

Adverse changes in global financial markets could limit our ability and our larger customers’ and suppliers’ ability to access capital or increase the cost of capital needed to fund business operations.

short_term.title: Short-term view (0–30 days · next earnings cycle)
bullets: Run-rate $31.51B in FY2026 Q1, +4.3% YoY — stable trajectory entering the next print.
Next-quarter consensus: $32.25B (+2.4% vs current Q) — set by 25 broker contributors.
EPS consensus next qtr: $3.15 vs current $3.00.
Margin context: 13.3% net margin (21.1% op margin) — stable.
Next earnings: 2026-08-11 — primary catalyst for the short-term window.
short_term.takeaway: Going into the next earnings print, the setup is constructive — consensus is achievable based on current run-rate.

long_term.title: Long-term view (90+ days · multi-year setup)
bullets: TTM revenue $124B — in cyclical drawdown.
Sell-side: FY1 $129B → FY2 $138B (+7.0% multi-year top-line implied).
Forward EBIT margin: 25.0% — average operating profile over the long-cycle view.
6-quarter run-rate CAGR: +4.6% — the secular trajectory the market is pricing.
FCF generation $8.8B/qtr funds long-term capital return optionality (buybacks/dividends/M&A).
Sector thesis (Communication Services): Ad spending normalization + AI-driven engagement reshaping the long-run growth profile.
long_term.takeaway: Long-cycle thesis is intact if the next 2–4 quarters confirm a stabilization in trajectory.

short_term.title: Short-term view (0–30 days · trading window)

MACD: -0.57 vs signal -0.50 — bearish crossover.

short_term.title: Short-term view (0–45 DTE · front-month tactical)
bullets: Front-month ATM IV: 48.3% — normal.
Put/Call OI: 0.46 — call-heavy positioning (consensus bullish).
30-day implied move: ±5.3% ($23.33 – $25.97) — the 1-σ range the options market is pricing.

long_term.title: Long-term view (90+ DTE · LEAPS / structural)
bullets: Back-month avg IV (90+ DTE): 26.3% across 8 expiries.
Available long-dated expiries: 8 dates spanning 98–616 DTE.
90-day implied move: ±13.6% ($21.31 – $27.99).
398-day implied move: ±27.9% ($17.77 – $31.53) — the long-dated 1-σ range.
Sell-side PT vs options-implied range: consensus $24 (-2.6% upside). Compare to the 90+ DTE implied range above.
Term structure in backwardation (-22.1pp front→back) — near-term event premium; long-dated vol cheaper.
long_term.strategy: Bear put spread 6-month: long $23 / short $21. Defined-cost downside positioning; cheaper than outright long puts; captures 10% further downside efficiently.
